gauge.name: Nimishillen Creek at North Industry

impacts.statement: FEMA 0.2 percent flood level. Multiple properties flooded in Canton and North Industry.
FEMA 1 percent flood level. Major flooding in North Industry and Howenstine. Homes on Shaker Valley Circle and Hillsboro Avenue cut off from flooding on Cheyenne Street to Ridge Road.
Water inundates the first floor of homes in Canton and North Industry. Central Avenue Southeast floods in Canton Township.

Widespread flooding in southern portions of Canton from Ridge Avenue north to Allenford Drive. Flood waters cut off access to homes along Cheyenne Street and Allenford Drive. Flood waters cut off access to properties on Cleveland Southwest Avenue. Some low lying properties inundated.
Water backs up into a mobile home park on Cleveland Southwest Avenue in Canton Township.
Low lying portions of Southeast Sparta Avenue and Allenford Drive threatened. Flood waters inundate recreational fields of Canton South High School.
Creek out of banks. Threatening low lying roads between North Industry and Howenstine.

hydronotes.statement: Stage may be affected by ice.

value.label: North American Vertical Datum of 1988
value.description: A fixed reference for elevations determined by geodetic leveling. The datum was derived from a general adjustment of the first-order terrestrial leveling nets of the United States, Canada, and Mexico. In the adjustment, only the height of the primary tidal bench mark, referenced to the International Great Lakes Datum of 1985 (IGLD 85) local mean sea level height value, at Father Point, Rimouski, Quebec, Canada was held fixed, thus providing minimum constraint. NAVD 88 and IGLD 85 are identical. However, NAVD 88 bench mark values are given in Helmert orthometric height units while IGLD 85 values are in dynamic heights. See International Great Lakes Datum of 1985, National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929, and geopotential difference. NAVD 88 should not be used as Mean Sea Level.

gauge.forecastReliability: Forecasts are issued as needed during times of high water, but are not routinely available.
